note.soap:
S: A 35-year-old male reports burning sensation when urinating without fever, flank pain, or testicular pain. No systemic symptoms reported. There are no known medication allergies. Pregnancy status is not captured. 

O: No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. Vital signs and physical findings cannot be assessed. No laboratory results available at this time.

A:  
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): UTI in male (complicated by definition)  
  - Differential: male dysuria—UTI vs urethritis (including gonorrhea/chlamydia) vs prostatitis  
  - Red flags considered: absence of fever, flank pain, and testicular pain reduces concern for pyelonephritis or severe prostatitis but cannot be excluded without exam and labs

P:  
  - Disposition: Urgent care or primary care visit recommended for urinalysis, urine culture, and STI testing to confirm diagnosis and guide therapy.  
  - Plan: Empiric antibiotic therapy may be considered after evaluation; avoid fluoroquinolones if local resistance is high or contraindications exist. Symptomatic treatment with hydration and analgesics advised.  
  - Safety net: Advise patient to seek immediate care if fever, worsening pain, testicular swelling, or systemic symptoms develop. Follow-up within 48–72 hours to review test results and clinical progress is essential.

Evidence (snippets used): None
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.
